name: Metztli
description: Metztli, also known as Coyolxauhqui in some interpretations, is the Aztec goddess of the moon. Her origin story is deeply intertwined with that of her brother, Huitzilopochtli, the god of war and the sun. According to legend, Coatlicue, their mother, became pregnant miraculously after tucking a ball of feathers into her bosom. Enraged by their mother's inexplicable pregnancy and suspecting infidelity, Metztli rallied her 400 brothers, the Centzonhuitznahua (the Southern Stars), to kill Coatlicue. However, Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed from his mother's womb and swiftly defeated his siblings, including Metztli. He dismembered her body and threw it down the mountain Coatepec, scattering her limbs. This act is believed to have created the moon, and some stories say her face can still be seen on its surface. As the moon goddess, Metztli governs the night, fertility, and the earth. She also represents femininity and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. Her phases are associated with the lunar cycle, and she is often invoked for guidance and protection. While her fate appears tragic, Metztli's role as the moon is essential in the Aztec cosmology, providing balance and contrast to the sun's dominance. She is a powerful symbol of resilience and the enduring power of the feminine spirit, even in the face of adversity. Offerings to Metztli often included rabbits, as the shape of a rabbit is sometimes seen on the surface of the moon.
subtitle: Aztec Goddess of the Moon

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Huitzilopochtli
keyMyths.description: Coatlicue's mysterious pregnancy enraged Metztli and her brothers, leading them to attempt to kill their mother. However, Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed and defended Coatlicue.

keyMyths.title: The Dismemberment of Metztli
keyMyths.description: After Huitzilopochtli's birth, he defeated Metztli and her brothers, dismembering her body and casting it down Coatepec mountain. This act is said to have created the moon.

keyMyths.title: The Rabbit in the Moon
keyMyths.description: Some stories say that the shape of a rabbit can be seen on the surface of the moon, associating Metztli with this animal. This is often depicted in Aztec art.

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings to Metztli often included nocturnal animals, such as owls and bats, as well as silver objects and seashells, reflecting her association with the night and the sea. These offerings were intended to appease her and ensure the moon's continued cycle.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Dances
worshipPractices.description: Ritual dances were performed in honor of Metztli, mimicking the phases of the moon and her journey across the night sky. Dancers wore silver ornaments and moon-shaped headdresses, symbolizing her celestial power and influence over fertility and growth.

festivals.name: Tozoztontli
festivals.description: During Tozoztontli, a festival dedicated to agricultural renewal, offerings were made to Metztli to ensure the fertility of the land and a bountiful harvest. Her influence over water and the lunar cycle was seen as vital for the growth of crops.

festivals.name: Hueytozoztli
festivals.description: Hueytozoztli involved more elaborate ceremonies and sacrifices, further emphasizing the importance of Metztli's role in the agricultural cycle. Prayers were offered for rain and the continued growth of maize, the staple crop of the Aztec civilization.

cultural.modernLegacy: While direct worship of Metztli is no longer practiced, her image and symbolism continue to appear in Mexican art, literature, and cultural celebrations. She represents feminine power, the cyclical nature of life, and the enduring connection between humanity and the cosmos. Her story is often invoked in feminist contexts.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Metztli's origin is closely tied to the myth of her mother, Coatlicue, and her brother, Huitzilopochtli. Coatlicue, while performing her duties at Coatepec (Snake Mountain), was impregnated by a ball of feathers that fell from the sky. Her existing children, including Metztli, were enraged by this perceived dishonor, believing their mother had been unfaithful. Metztli, as one of the leaders of this rebellion, plotted to kill Coatlicue. However, Huitzilopochtli was miraculously born fully armed from Coatlicue's womb and swiftly defeated his siblings, including Metztli. This event is not just a story of betrayal and vengeance, but also a symbolic representation of the cosmic struggle between the sun and the moon, day and night. Some interpretations identify Metztli with Coyolxauhqui, the leader of the rebellious siblings who was dismembered by Huitzilopochtli, further emphasizing the moon's cyclical phases.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The primary myth associated with Metztli revolves around her conflict with her brother, Huitzilopochtli. After Huitzilopochtli's birth and victory, Metztli's fate varies depending on the version of the myth. In some accounts, she is decapitated and her body scattered across the sky, representing the phases of the moon. This dismemberment symbolizes the moon's waxing and waning, constantly changing form. Other accounts suggest that after her defeat, she was cast into the sky to become the moon, forever chasing her brother, the sun, across the heavens. This eternal chase explains why the moon is only visible at night, when the sun is absent. These narratives highlight the themes of sibling rivalry, cosmic order, and the cyclical nature of existence that were central to Aztec cosmology.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While not as prominent as the worship of Huitzilopochtli or Tlaloc, Metztli held a significant position in the Aztec pantheon and was revered for her influence over the night, water, and fertility. Her temples, though less grand than those of the major deities, were places of reverence where priests and priestesses conducted rituals and made offerings. These rituals often involved nocturnal animals, such as owls and bats, which were associated with the moon's domain. Furthermore, seashells and silver objects were considered sacred to Metztli, reflecting her connection to the sea and the precious metal associated with the lunar cycle. The timing of agricultural ceremonies was often aligned with the lunar phases, recognizing Metztli's vital role in ensuring successful harvests and the continuation of life.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Metztli's legacy extends beyond her role as a deity within the Aztec pantheon. She represents the feminine principle, the cyclical nature of time, and the enduring power of the moon. Her story, particularly the myth of her conflict with Huitzilopochtli, serves as a cautionary tale about the consequences of discord and the importance of maintaining cosmic balance. In modern Mexico, Metztli's image and symbolism continue to appear in art, literature, and cultural celebrations, often representing indigenous identity and resistance. Her association with fertility and the natural world also resonates with contemporary environmental movements. The enduring presence of Metztli in Mexican culture demonstrates the lasting impact of Aztec mythology and the power of ancient beliefs to inspire and inform contemporary society.

shortDescription: Aztec moon goddess. Sister of Huitzilopochtli, her story is intertwined with the moon's cycles and night.
